sentences.zh: 諺曰「力田不如逢年，善仕不如遇合」，固無虛言。

translations.literal: A proverb says, 'Toiling at farming is not as good as encountering a good year; being skilled at officialdom is not as good as meeting the right opportunity.' These are certainly not empty words.
translations.idiomatic: As the proverb goes, "Hard work in the fields counts for less than a good harvest year, and skill in office counts for less than finding the right patron." These words ring true indeed.

sentences.zh: 非獨女以色媚，而士宦亦有之。

translations.literal: It is not only women who ingratiate themselves through beauty; officials and eunuchs also have such people among them.
translations.idiomatic: It is not only women who win favor through their looks—men in service at court have done the same.

sentences.zh: 昔以色幸者多矣。

translations.literal: In the past, those who were favored through their appearance were many.
translations.idiomatic: In times past, many rose to favor on the strength of their beauty.

sentences.zh: 至漢興，高祖至暴抗也，然籍孺以佞幸；

translations.literal: When the Han dynasty arose, Gaozu was most violent and domineering, yet Ji Ru gained favor through flattery and charm;
translations.idiomatic: When the Han dynasty was founded, Emperor Gaozu was as fierce and unyielding a man as ever lived, yet even he kept Ji Ru as a favorite through flattery and charm.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時有閎孺。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaohui, there was Hong Ru.
translations.idiomatic: During the reign of Emperor Hui, there was Hong Ru.

sentences.zh: 此兩人非有材能，徒以婉佞貴幸，與上臥起，公卿皆因關說。

translations.literal: These two men had no talent or ability; they were honored and favored solely through their suppliant flattery, sharing the emperor's bed and board, and all the ministers and nobles sought to intercede through them.
translations.idiomatic: Neither of these two men possessed any talent whatsoever. They rose to honor and favor solely through their ingratiating ways, sharing the emperor's bed, and every minister and noble at court sought to press their petitions through them.

sentences.zh: 故孝惠時郎侍中皆冠鵕鸃，貝帶，傅脂粉，化閎、籍之屬也。

translations.literal: Thus during Emperor Xiaohui's reign, all the palace gentlemen and attendants wore caps adorned with junyi bird feathers, belts decorated with cowrie shells, and applied rouge and powder, imitating the fashion of Hong Ru, Ji Ru, and their kind.
translations.idiomatic: And so during Emperor Hui's reign, every palace gentleman and attendant at court wore caps adorned with junyi feathers and belts studded with cowrie shells, their faces powdered and rouged—all in imitation of Hong Ru and Ji Ru.

sentences.zh: 兩人徙家安陵。

translations.literal: The two men relocated their families to Anling.
translations.idiomatic: Both men moved their households to Anling.

sentences.zh: 孝文時中寵臣，士人則鄧通，宦者則趙同、北宮伯子。

translations.literal: Among the inner court favorites during Emperor Xiaowen's reign, among gentlemen there was Deng Tong, and among eunuchs there were Zhao Tong and Beigong Bozi.
translations.idiomatic: Among the favored intimates at Emperor Wen's court, the gentleman was Deng Tong, and the eunuchs were Zhao Tong and Beigong Bozi.

sentences.zh: 北宮伯子以愛人長者；

translations.literal: Beigong Bozi was favored because he loved others and was a dignified elder;
translations.idiomatic: Beigong Bozi won favor for his kindness and dignified bearing.

sentences.zh: 而趙同以星氣幸，常為文帝參乘；

translations.literal: Zhao Tong was favored through his knowledge of astrology and the study of vapors, and he regularly served as Emperor Wen's chariot companion;
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Tong found favor through his knowledge of astrology and divination by cloud formations, and he regularly rode beside Emperor Wen in the imperial chariot.

sentences.zh: 鄧通無伎能。

translations.literal: Deng Tong had no skills or abilities.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong possessed no particular skill or talent.

sentences.zh: 鄧通，蜀郡南安人也，以濯船為黃頭郎。

translations.literal: Deng Tong was a man of Nan'an in the Commandery of Shu, who served as a Yellow-Cap Boatman by rowing boats.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong hailed from Nan'an in the Commandery of Shu. He had entered palace service as a Yellow-Cap Boatman, poling vessels along the imperial waterways.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝夢欲上天，不能，有一黃頭郎從後推之上天，顧見其衣裻帶后穿。

translations.literal: Emperor Xiaowen dreamed that he wanted to ascend to heaven but could not, when a Yellow-Cap Boatman pushed him from behind up to heaven. Looking back, he saw that the man's robe had a hole in the seam at the back.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen once dreamed that he was striving to ascend to heaven but could not reach it, when a Yellow-Cap Boatman came up behind him and pushed him aloft. Glancing back, the emperor noticed that the man's robe was torn at the seam in the back.

sentences.zh: 覺而之漸臺，以夢中陰目求推者郎，即見鄧通，其衣后穿，夢中所見也。

translations.literal: After waking, he went to the Jiantai terrace, and secretly searched with his eyes among the attendants for the man who had pushed him in the dream. He immediately spotted Deng Tong, whose robe was torn in the back—just as he had seen in the dream.
translations.idiomatic: When he woke, the emperor made his way to the Jian Terrace and quietly scanned the attendants, searching for the man from his dream. His gaze fell at once on Deng Tong, whose robe was torn at the back—exactly as he had seen it in the dream.

sentences.zh: 召問其名姓，姓鄧氏，名通，文帝說焉，尊幸之日異。

translations.literal: He summoned the man and asked his name and surname. His surname was Deng, his given name Tong. Emperor Wen was pleased, and honored and favored him more with each passing day.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned the man and asked his name. His surname was Deng, his given name Tong. Emperor Wen was delighted, and from that day forward his favor toward Deng Tong only grew.

sentences.zh: 通亦願謹，不好外交，雖賜洗沐，不欲出。

translations.literal: Tong was also careful and prudent, not fond of socializing outside. Even when granted leave days for bathing, he did not wish to go out.
translations.idiomatic: For his part, Tong was circumspect and cautious, with no taste for outside socializing. Even on his leave days for bathing, he preferred not to go out.

sentences.zh: 於是文帝賞賜通巨萬以十數，官至上大夫。

translations.literal: Thereupon Emperor Wen bestowed rewards on Tong amounting to tens of millions, and his official rank reached Senior Grand Master.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen showered him with gifts totaling tens of millions of cash, and raised his rank to Senior Grand Master.

sentences.zh: 文帝時時如鄧通家遊戲。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen would from time to time go to Deng Tong's home to amuse himself.
translations.idiomatic: From time to time, Emperor Wen would visit Deng Tong's residence to amuse himself.

sentences.zh: 然鄧通無他能，不能有所薦士，獨自謹其身以媚上而已。

translations.literal: Yet Deng Tong had no other ability and could not recommend any scholars for office; he merely conducted himself carefully to please the emperor, and nothing more.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Deng Tong had no other talent and never recommended a single person for office. He simply took care to conduct himself properly and please the emperor—nothing more.

sentences.zh: 上使善相者相通，曰「當貧餓死」。

translations.literal: The emperor had a skilled physiognomist examine Tong's face, who said, 'He is destined to die of poverty and starvation.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned a skilled physiognomist to read Tong's face. The verdict was grim: "This man is fated to die in poverty, of starvation."

sentences.zh: 文帝曰：「能富通者在我也。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen said, 'The power to make Tong rich lies with me.'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen said, "The power to make Tong rich rests with me."

sentences.zh: 何謂貧乎？」

translations.literal: How can one speak of poverty?
translations.idiomatic: "How could he ever become poor?"

sentences.zh: 於是賜鄧通蜀嚴道銅山，得自鑄錢，「鄧氏錢」布天下。

translations.literal: Thereupon he granted Deng Tong the copper mountains at Yandao in Shu, with permission to mint his own coins. 'Deng clan coins' spread throughout the empire.
translations.idiomatic: Thereupon he granted Deng Tong the copper mines at Yandao in Shu, with the right to mint his own coins. The "Deng coins" soon circulated throughout the empire.

sentences.zh: 其富如此。

translations.literal: Such was the extent of his wealth.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the measure of his wealth.

sentences.zh: 文帝嘗病癰，鄧通常為帝唶吮之。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen once suffered from an abscess, and Deng Tong would regularly suck the pus from it for the emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Once Emperor Wen developed an abscess, and Deng Tong would regularly suck the infected wound clean for him.

sentences.zh: 文帝不樂，從容問通曰：「天下誰最愛我者乎？」

translations.literal: Emperor Wen, feeling unhappy, casually asked Tong, 'Who in all the realm loves me the most?'
translations.idiomatic: One day, feeling low, Emperor Wen asked Tong offhandedly, "Who in all the realm loves me the most?"

sentences.zh: 通曰：「宜莫如太子。」

translations.literal: Tong said, 'Surely none more so than the Crown Prince.'
translations.idiomatic: Tong replied, "Surely none more than the Crown Prince."

sentences.zh: 太子入問病，文帝使唶癰，唶癰而色難之。

translations.literal: When the Crown Prince came in to inquire about the emperor's illness, Emperor Wen had him suck the abscess. He sucked the abscess, but his expression showed his difficulty and reluctance.
translations.idiomatic: When the Crown Prince came to inquire about the emperor's illness, Emperor Wen bade him suck the abscess. The prince did so, but his face betrayed his disgust.

sentences.zh: 已而聞鄧通常為帝唶吮之，心慚，由此怨通矣。

translations.literal: Afterward, when he heard that Deng Tong regularly sucked the abscess for the emperor, he felt ashamed in his heart, and from this point on bore a grudge against Tong.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when the Crown Prince learned that Deng Tong had been doing this all along without complaint, he was filled with shame, and from that day bore a bitter grudge against Tong.

sentences.zh: 及文帝崩，景帝立，鄧通免，家居。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen passed away and Emperor Jing was enthroned, Deng Tong was dismissed from office and stayed at home.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Wen died and Emperor Jing took the throne, Deng Tong was stripped of his offices and sent home.

sentences.zh: 居無何，人有告鄧通盜出徼外鑄錢。

translations.literal: Before long, someone accused Deng Tong of secretly smuggling coins beyond the frontier for minting.
translations.idiomatic: Before long, someone reported that Deng Tong had been illegally minting coins and smuggling them beyond the frontier.

sentences.zh: 下吏驗問，頗有之，遂竟案，盡沒入鄧通家，尚負責數巨萬。

translations.literal: The case was referred to officials for investigation, and considerable evidence was found. The case was pursued to its conclusion, and all of Deng Tong's household property was confiscated, yet he still owed debts of tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: The matter was handed to the officials for investigation. Substantial evidence was found, and the case was prosecuted to its end. The entire Deng household was stripped of its property, yet Tong still owed debts amounting to tens of millions of cash.

sentences.zh: 長公主賜鄧通，吏輒隨沒入之，一簪不得著身。

translations.literal: The Elder Princess gave gifts to Deng Tong, but officials would immediately follow and confiscate them, so that not a single hairpin was allowed to remain on his person.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the Elder Princess sent gifts to Deng Tong, officials would swoop in at once to confiscate them. He was not allowed to keep so much as a hairpin.

sentences.zh: 於是長公主乃令假衣食。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Elder Princess arranged to lend him clothing and food.
translations.idiomatic: In the end, the Elder Princess could only arrange to lend him clothes and food.

sentences.zh: 竟不得名一錢，寄死人家。

translations.literal: In the end he could not call a single coin his own, and died as a dependent in another person's household.
translations.idiomatic: He ended his days without a single coin to his name, dying as a dependent in someone else's household.

sentences.zh: 孝景帝時，中無寵臣，然獨郎中令周文仁，仁寵最過庸，乃不甚篤。

translations.literal: During Emperor Jing's reign, there were no inner court favorites. Only the Palace Gentleman Commander Zhou Wren enjoyed favor beyond his station, but even his favor was not very deep.
translations.idiomatic: During Emperor Jing's reign, there were no notable court favorites. The only exception was Zhou Wren, the Palace Gentleman Commander, whose favor somewhat exceeded his merits, though even that was nothing remarkable.

sentences.zh: 今天子中寵臣，士人則韓王孫嫣，宦者則李延年。

translations.literal: Among the current emperor's inner court favorites, among gentlemen there is Han Yan, grandson of the King of Han, and among eunuchs there is Li Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: Among the current emperor's favored intimates, the gentleman is Han Yan, a scion of the former Kings of Han, and the eunuch is Li Yannian.

sentences.zh: 嫣者，弓高侯孽孫也。

translations.literal: Yan was a collateral grandson of the Marquis of Gonggao.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan was a collateral grandson of the Marquis of Gonggao.

sentences.zh: 今上為膠東王時，嫣與上學書相愛。

translations.literal: When the current emperor was King of Jiaodong, Yan studied calligraphy with the emperor and the two became close.
translations.idiomatic: When the current emperor was still the King of Jiaodong, Yan studied with him, and the two grew deeply attached.

sentences.zh: 及上為太子，愈益親嫣。

translations.literal: When the emperor became Crown Prince, he became ever more intimate with Yan.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor became Crown Prince, his intimacy with Yan only deepened.

sentences.zh: 嫣善騎射，善佞。

translations.literal: Yan was skilled at riding and archery, and skilled at flattery.
translations.idiomatic: Yan excelled at riding and archery, and was adept at the art of flattery.

sentences.zh: 上即位，欲事伐匈奴，而嫣先習胡兵，以故益尊貴，官至上大夫，賞賜擬於鄧通。

translations.literal: When the emperor ascended the throne, he wished to undertake campaigns against the Xiongnu, and Yan had already practiced the arts of barbarian warfare. For this reason his rank and honor increased further, his official rank reached Senior Grand Master, and the rewards bestowed on him were comparable to those of Deng Tong.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor took the throne and resolved to wage war against the Xiongnu, Yan's prior training in the military arts of the steppe peoples made him all the more valued. His rank rose to Senior Grand Master, and the gifts he received rivaled those once lavished on Deng Tong.

sentences.zh: 時嫣常與上臥起。

translations.literal: At that time, Yan regularly shared the emperor's bed and board.
translations.idiomatic: In those days, Yan shared the emperor's bed and table.

sentences.zh: 江都王入朝，有詔得從入獵上林中。

translations.literal: The King of Jiangdu came to court, and received an edict permitting him to accompany the emperor on a hunt in the Shanglin Park.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Jiangdu came to court on an audience visit and was granted an imperial edict allowing him to join the emperor's hunt in the Shanglin Park.

sentences.zh: 天子車駕蹕道未行，而先使嫣乘副車，從數十百騎，騖馳視獸。

translations.literal: Before the emperor's chariot and procession had set out along the cleared road, he first sent Yan ahead riding in a secondary imperial chariot, attended by scores of mounted horsemen, galloping to scout the game.
translations.idiomatic: Before the emperor's own chariot had even set out along the cleared road, he sent Yan ahead in a secondary imperial carriage, attended by scores of mounted riders, galloping through the park to scout the game.

sentences.zh: 江都王望見，以為天子，辟從者，伏謁道傍。

translations.literal: The King of Jiangdu, seeing this from afar, took it for the emperor. He dismissed his attendants and prostrated himself in greeting by the roadside.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Jiangdu saw this procession from a distance and, mistaking it for the emperor's own, waved his retinue aside and prostrated himself in greeting by the roadside.

sentences.zh: 嫣驅不見。

translations.literal: Yan drove past without acknowledging him.
translations.idiomatic: Yan swept past without so much as a glance.

sentences.zh: 既過，江都王怒，為皇太后泣曰：「請得歸國入宿衛，比韓嫣。」

translations.literal: After the party had passed, the King of Jiangdu was furious. He wept before the Empress Dowager and said, 'I beg permission to return my kingdom and enter the Palace Guard, so as to be treated like Han Yan.'
translations.idiomatic: After the procession had passed, the King of Jiangdu was livid. He went weeping before the Empress Dowager and said, "I beg leave to surrender my kingdom and join the Palace Guard, if only to be treated as well as Han Yan."

sentences.zh: 太后由此嗛嫣。

translations.literal: The Empress Dowager from this point bore a grudge against Yan.
translations.idiomatic: From that moment, the Empress Dowager harbored a deep resentment toward Yan.

sentences.zh: 嫣侍上，出入永巷不禁，以姦聞皇太后。

translations.literal: Yan, in his attendance upon the emperor, came and went through the Yongxiang palace women's quarters without restriction. Reports of his illicit liaisons reached the Empress Dowager.
translations.idiomatic: In his attendance upon the emperor, Yan came and went freely through the Yongxiang, the palace women's quarters, without restriction. Word of his illicit affairs reached the Empress Dowager.

sentences.zh: 皇太后怒，使使賜嫣死。

translations.literal: The Empress Dowager was furious and sent a messenger to bestow death upon Yan.
translations.idiomatic: The Empress Dowager was furious and dispatched a messenger to order Yan to take his own life.

sentences.zh: 上為謝，終不能得，嫣遂死。

translations.literal: The emperor pleaded on Yan's behalf, but in the end could not obtain a pardon. Yan thereupon died.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor pleaded for clemency, but the Empress Dowager would not relent, and Yan was put to death.

sentences.zh: 而案道侯韓說，其弟也，亦佞幸。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Andao, Han Yue, was Yan's younger brother, and he too gained favor through flattery.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yue, the Marquis of Andao, was Yan's younger brother, and he too became a court favorite.

sentences.zh: 李延年，中山人也。

translations.literal: Li Yannian was a man from Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yannian hailed from the kingdom of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 父母及身兄弟及女，皆故倡也。

translations.literal: His parents, he himself, his brothers, and his sisters had all formerly been entertainers.
translations.idiomatic: His parents, he himself, his brothers, and his sisters had all been entertainers.

sentences.zh: 延年坐法腐，給事狗中。

translations.literal: Yannian was convicted of a crime and punished with castration, and was assigned to serve in the dog kennels.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian was convicted of a crime and sentenced to castration, after which he was set to work in the palace dog kennels.

sentences.zh: 而平陽公主言延年女弟善舞，上見，心說之，及入永巷，而召貴延年。

translations.literal: Princess Pingyang mentioned that Yannian's younger sister was skilled at dancing. The emperor saw her and was pleased. After she entered the Yongxiang palace quarters, the emperor summoned and elevated Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: Princess Pingyang mentioned that Yannian's younger sister was a gifted dancer. When the emperor saw her perform, he was captivated. After she was taken into the palace, the emperor summoned Yannian and raised him to a position of honor.

sentences.zh: 延年善歌，為變新聲，而上方興天地祠，欲造樂詩歌弦之。

translations.literal: Yannian was skilled at singing and at composing new melodies. The emperor was at that time promoting sacrifices to Heaven and Earth, and wished to create musical poems and songs to be set to stringed instruments.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian was a gifted singer and composer of new melodies. At that time the emperor was instituting grand sacrifices to Heaven and Earth, and wished to commission poems and hymns to be set to music for the ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 延年善承意，弦次初詩。

translations.literal: Yannian was skilled at divining the emperor's wishes, and set the newly composed poems to music in sequence.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian had a gift for anticipating the emperor's wishes, and deftly set the newly composed poems to music.

sentences.zh: 其女弟亦幸，有子男。

translations.literal: His younger sister was also favored by the emperor, and bore a son.
translations.idiomatic: His younger sister likewise became an imperial favorite and bore the emperor a son.

sentences.zh: 延年佩二千石印，號協聲律。

translations.literal: Yannian wore the seal of a two-thousand-bushel official, and held the title of Harmonizer of Tones and Pitches.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian was granted the seal of a two-thousand-bushel official and bore the title Harmonizer of Tones and Pitches.

sentences.zh: 與上臥起，甚貴幸，埒如韓嫣也。

translations.literal: He shared the emperor's bed and board, and was greatly honored and favored, on a par with Han Yan.
translations.idiomatic: He shared the emperor's bed and table, honored and favored to a degree that rivaled Han Yan himself.

sentences.zh: 久之，寖與中人亂，出入驕恣。

translations.literal: As time passed, he gradually became involved in licentious affairs with palace women, and grew arrogant and unrestrained in his comings and goings.
translations.idiomatic: In time, he began carrying on illicit affairs with the palace women and grew ever more brazen and unrestrained.

sentences.zh: 及其女弟李夫人卒后，愛弛，則禽誅延年昆弟也。

translations.literal: After his younger sister, Lady Li, died, the emperor's affection waned, and Yannian and his brothers were arrested and executed.
translations.idiomatic: After his sister, Lady Li, died, the emperor's affection cooled, and Yannian and his brothers were arrested and put to death.

sentences.zh: 自是之后，內寵嬖臣大底外戚之家，然不足數也。

translations.literal: After this, the inner court favorites and beloved ministers were mostly from the families of imperial consorts, but they were not worth enumerating.
translations.idiomatic: From this time on, the court favorites were for the most part drawn from the families of imperial consorts, but they were hardly worth mentioning.

sentences.zh: 衛青、霍去病亦以外戚貴幸，然頗用材能自進。

translations.literal: Wei Qing and Huo Qubing also rose to honor and favor through their status as relatives of the imperial consort, but they relied considerably on their own talent and ability to advance.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing and Huo Qubing likewise rose to prominence through their ties to the imperial consort's family, yet they owed much of their advancement to genuine talent and ability.

sentences.zh: 太史公曰：甚哉愛憎之時！

translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: How extreme is the fickleness of love and hate!
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: How swiftly love turns to loathing!

sentences.zh: 彌子瑕之行，足以觀后人佞幸矣。

translations.literal: The story of Mi Zixia's conduct is sufficient to illustrate the fate of court favorites for later generations.
translations.idiomatic: The tale of Mi Zixia tells all that later ages need to know about the fate of imperial favorites.

sentences.zh: 雖百世可知也。

translations.literal: Even after a hundred generations, this can be known.
translations.idiomatic: Even a hundred generations hence, the pattern will be plain to see.
